reviews.review_text:
Such a disappointment. The game starts off very very well, controls are good, weapons are fun to use, and an interesting upgrade system. (SPOILERS BELOW)

However, once you escape the 1st area and get into the "open world" areas, the game takes a dive.
Why? Infinitely (and quickly) respawning enemies. You destroy a camera, a flying robot comes to fix it. Same with enemies. It takes LITERALLY less than ONE second for the flying robots to come to rebuild the cameras you break down. 
If you kill the flying robots before they reach the cameras? THEY JUST KEEP RESPAWNING.

They basically built a beautiful open area, THEORETICALLY inviting you to explore and roam, however made the game so hostile towards the player that you give up on exploring, the constant and nonstop respawning goes diametrically against the exploration and wandering around.
You run out of patience, and avoid all other buildings (which contains blueprints) because the game makes its damnedest sure to make enemies annoying as possible, all the time, everywhere.

After seeing the open world area, I would've preferred to be stuck in corridors in the complex, where there was no infinitely respawning enemies.

And if the objetctive of this design is just to make you run away from everything....why have open areas in the first place?

I think this is the first game I have ever played that is actively designed to make you NOT want to explore its world.

And that's the worst part. IT was a great great game until it opened up. Such a downfall.

Quit joking, let's take this game more seriously.
Amazing gameplay & Amazing OST: a few words about Atomic Heart. Even on Medium difficulty, enemies not so easy to beat.
Honor to mention: Style of retrofuturism took me in to the heart, especially this atmosphere of XX century on USSR. My parents told me that living in Soviet Union was challenging, just like in Russia today, but without high technologies.
I've made a lot of screenshots of locations i like, every of them feels like masterpiece. I see how much time and care ppl from Mundfish spent on task to make this beautiful
IMHO, people shouldn't play Atomic Heart without story DLCs, because this is a huge part of story that people can miss and fail to grasp the depth of the plot.


About some things that i don't like.
1. Game is not available on Russian/Kazakh region of Steam. Some people were forced to play on VK Play (♥♥♥♥♥♥ alternative of Steam, even worse that Epic Games), because there is no way to buy it legally from these regions. 
2. Bugs. Game turned 3 years old, but still has some childish mistakes and bugs. Trapped in Limbo DLC is a great example. No, i did not get my game crashed, but i was forced to restart it. Physics (fundamental thing on which is based the whole DLC) was broken after some save reloads. 
3. Achievements. Some of them are impossible to beat without retake whole story mode. Example, achievements "The Necromancer" and "Burning Ears". You can't go back to Vavilov and the village after completing this chapters. You should restart, or if you completed story first, you need to play New Game+
(Tip: [spoiler]if you want to complete "Atomic Heart" achievement without pain in ass, complete the game on the first time on lower difficulty and play New Game+, you will get all of your equipment from the last save[/spoiler])

Atomic heart is a beast of a game.

Soviet Utopia, Futuristic automation, Robots and communism.

Story-Rich Single-Player, Engaging conversations, Wonderful visuals and character designs, And also, Good witty humor.

Gives the perfect blend on soviet ultra-visionary future, Story expands to multiple DLCs and every single one of them is a must to continue the story, Probably not trapped in limbo, It's an odd DLC, But yet worth it for me.

All characters are loveable especially blesna, P-3 and especially, Somadelkin, That gym rat of a robot.

Also nastya with her dolphin-centered knowledge, Which has some funny honest conversations between her and NORA in the final DLC in the crystal complex, Funny ones.

If you like Fallout-4 game elements, With soviet futurism, This game is a must-buy, Awaiting Atomic Heart 2 whenever they decide to release it, This is one of my most favorite games, Like the witcher 3 wild hunt, If the combat is repetitive, The story is addicting.

Honestly kind of annoying game. It follows the trope that neither the main character, nor the ... "partner" never just seem to be quiet. It feels like it hand holds over voice to such a degree that it feels like the game thinks I am a child or someone who can't grasp simple text instructions, or learn anything from the game from the environment. Gameplay was fine. Crafting feels a bit forced at times.

Among some of my favorite game franchises like Fallout and Bioshock I've always had this itch for a game that took me back when I first played those games. Getting the retrofuturistic formula right carries of a lot of creativity and ingenuity with it as real world interpretations from a now past futuristic setting comes in all forms shapes and distinctive takes.

It isn't that surprising that upon seeing the Atomic Heart E3 reveal trailer in 2018 my itch was cured and I had a game that I was looking forward to after playing fallout:new vegas, 4, 76, 3, and 1 and 2 as well as every bioshock game in the franchise. These being franchises which are integral to my personality and interests. While I would like to further detail what Atomic heart get's right (including dlc) point by point by point by point like any other generic review I would just like to preface how absolutely IMPRESSED I was. Atomic heart ABSOLUTELY met my expectations in every single aspect like combat, music, visuals just everything. This game took me back to when I was a teenager in an era in gaming where everything was simpler and for pure fun. 

In a era without a new Fallout, or Bioshock. This is the PERFECT game to satisfy this itch. I love you MUNDFISH and you guys absolutely killed it with this game, so much so I literally bought this game twice one on steam and the xbox app.  I am GENUINELY looking forward to ATOMIC HEART II. Thank you for taking me back to when I first played some of my forever favorite gaming franchises. Oh I'm also pretty sure this is the game i've ever replayed the MOST.
Total masterpiece Comrades! Soviet Fallout universe with a doomfenstein gameplay!

The combat is really janky, the puzzles use really broken physics sometimes, the story is a bit interesting but the gameplay is such a slog I can't keep playing. Also english translations sound super unnatural so if you do decide to play, I'd recommend playing in russian w/ subtitles on instead

He routinely exclaims how crazy everything is, incase you did not realize how crazy everything is.  The other characters sound like your friends making fun of different national accents.

The game expects you to be out-of-your-mind bored, so the narrator reminds you in simple terms what you are supposed to be doing and there's a waypoint on your screen at all times which points you to whatever item solves the current puzzle you are supposed to be doing, if you consider bring the item to the box a puzzle. 

They should get the robots to play the game for you and it would be perfect.

As a player who enjoys light combat, and to follow a story, this isnt for me. The fights are WAY too hard, even in "easy" mode. I guess ill mod my way through this one, because I do enjoy the story, visuals and overall game. Im just not fond of the literal impossible enemies. Yes ive upgraded my skills, weapons etc.  but these fights are outrageous.

### Positives:
- The Gameplay, the Visuals, the game mechanics and the ease of adapting to different playstyles are all the reasons one should definitely try out this game 👍

- The devs really made the game easy to adapt to with how to allow you to change your builds quite easily in game so I really liked that point.

### Negatives:
- One thing which I disliked the most was the infinite waves of enemy. no matter how many times you kill some enemies in open world, they will be fixed by infinite number of repair bots. better to move along your way once you kill a machine and do not come back.

- Also, the story might feel a liitle lackluster or boderline nieche; like you'd immediately guess in few hours, who's the bad guy and who's not.

Edit: I was wrong about the story. The later parts of the game and the endings add some interesting twists. In the end, there isn't a clear good guy or bad guy—everyone is pursuing their own agenda, and even the character I sided with didn't exactly have noble intentions. The moral ambiguity was surprisingly well done and made the story more engaging than I initially expected.

This game was pure joy for me to play and watch. 
PROS : 
-It reminded me of Bioshock and Wolfenstein series beloved over the top oldschool story fps games. 

- The gameplay is nothing groundbreaking but that's exactly why I love it, back to basics! 

-Game runs smooth as butter.

- The visuals and world building is somethimes breath taking and so unique. Extra point for The twin designs :p Animations pretty well done.

- Music is fire!

- Story wise, I was so invested that I found myself just wanting to blast through the game to see what will happen next.

CONS:
- The most lackluster thing gameplaywise for me was the lack of audio feedback when you deal damage. You are shooting big clunky robots, I should hear and feel the impact of the hits. Also some enemies were moving so fast I couldn't f track them... I don't know, you may call it a skill issue :p

- There is a ragdoll effect and some enemies can just stunlock you witch is f annoying, and sometimes almost made me quit. The giant crabs that shield themselves in the DLC was a f atrocious enemy to fight.

- My last complain is the Limbo DLC. I just couldn't finish it, I had to skip that one. It was confusing, and annoying after a couple of minutes. I do appreciate the music and the platforming elements in it. 

100% RECOMMEND IT

[h1]Worth The Effort - Although you may not think so whilst playing It[/h1]

[h2] First Off... [/h2]
First off, this game is hard.  I mean extremely hard in places.  It makes no apologies for ramping up the chaos and seeing you die again and again.  It doesn’t spoon-feed you or take it easy on your fragile character at the start.  Or anywhere to be honest.  You earn every reward you get.

This “school of hard knocks” approach means each victory, upgrade, or just scraping through, feels something to be celebrated.

OK it’s not a souls-like, but there are a few areas where the save points were pretty sparse, and ammo, health and consumables ran very short (I’ll get to that in a bit).

[h2]Pros & Cons[/h2]

[h3]Pros[/h3]

[h3]Graphics[/h3] The way Atomic Heart carries its own “look” is nothing less than majestic.
From the interior corridors and abandoned labs etc., to the outdoor traversing across lush countryside and flying, it’s a really immersive world.

I had several “Oh my goodness.  Just look at that view or scene” moments, many of which are my desktop wallpaper.  And they manage to get across the scale of things extremely well.  Big robots look like BIG ROBOTS.  Monoliths look like monoliths.

The huge variety of robots and other foe, all look stunning too, with deadly robots and organic baddies, all having so much detail it’s jaw-dropping.

The human’s though?  Not so much.  Other than your character and the main story characters, NPC  humans generally look almost low-polygon.  Having said that, almost anyone you have to deal with has obviously had more time spent on them, and not a corpse in a chair, behind a window, that you pass in milliseconds.

[h3]Gameplay[/h3] Controls are simple and effective, with all the usual options to use controller or M&K.

Foes are varied and require particular strategies to overcome.  “Spray and pray” will not work. Although, “spray and set fire to” does.  This is particularly true with the boss fights, where, once you die (and you will), you can evaluate what would work better.

As with foes, locations and strategies, the weapon variety is pretty good. Although you only notice this as you build and craft them.  They’re accurate and can really pack a punch, and you can disassemble and craft bigger boom-sticks with the right ingredients.

The voice acting is extremely good and most speakers sounded natural.  No “Och ma wee laddie.  Those robots will burn my haggis, the noo.” efforts.

[h3]The story[/h3] is [b]Bloody Brilliant[/b] and has real depth and jeopardy.  The Russian designs and propaganda really sell the reasons why you’re doing what you’re doing, why robots and others are looking to kill you, and all the shenanigans behind the scenes. 

[i]Caveat to this is that the main cutscenes go on way too long and the disclosure moments are mostly dreary.[/i]

[h2]Cons[/h2]

[h3]Difficulty[/h3] Although there are options to lower the difficulty, this doesn’t make things a walk in the park.  Foes will still kick your backside and you will spend a lot of time running away. This difficulty is evident by how many players get various achievements, and seeing that less than 45% had completed the first main missions.

[h3]Learning curve.[/h3]  I felt that I was met with some serious situations without the necessary kit.  And that the early boss fights came out of nowhere.  Which just ramped things from L1 to L10. I felt I was using spitballs against a tank at times.  Obviously you upgrade your weapons and get more badass, but I felt you were hit with some enemies that jarred.

[h3]Interminable cutscenes.[/h3]  Too long in many places.

[h3]Vending[/h3] Although serviceable; buying, upgrading and crafting seemed extremely clumsy to start with.  It soon becomes second nature, but was (initially) annoying.  As does the “chat” the machine give you.  *Hint* You ALWAYS need more ammo.

[h2] In Conclusion [/h2]

Bloody brilliant game with awesome graphics, great story, memorable baddies and engrossing game-world.

Hard, but well worth the effort.  Look up a few “Things I wish I’d know before playing” on YouTube, just so you get the best experience.

I’ve dropped straight into the DLC.  That’s how good this game is.  87%

Russian Bioshock.  That's not the problem I have with the game.  Far from it.  The setting is interesting, the writing is fine, and the gameplay is decent enough.  A bit of a rocky start at the beginning, but largely fine...
Right up until you get to the outside areas.
Cameras everywhere.  Try to avoid the cameras, run into wandering robots.  Cameras catch you fighting, infinite robots summoned.  Take out the cameras?  They're repaired in about 30 seconds by flying repair bots.  Not after you leave the area, which would have been sensible, but under a minute while you're still standing there.  Take out the repair bots?  Well, you can't because they're infinitely spawned from an invulnerable pod.  At least the fliers summoned in the indoor sections are limited.  You can exhaust their supply (which is very helpfully shown on the wall).
Best way to deal with the outdoor sections is unfortunately to just ignore them.  Beeline to the next indoor area, and totally skip the large (and really quite nice looking) outdoor environments full of places to explore and things to find.  Or, there would be places to explore and things to find if you could get a moment's respite from the swarms of robots hunting you.

If you want to play the game for just the indoor areas, there's still probably a good chunk of game there.  I've definitely spent more time playing (and actually enjoying) the indoor areas than the outdoor areas, but really, with such a large chunk of the game seemingly being specifically designed to discourage its use... I just can't recommend.

Overall, I have to give the experience a thumbs down. It is an interesting game ... not an enjoyable one, in my opinion.

The story kind doesn't really grip you. It's your standard "robots went crazy, but actually humans are to blame" stuff ... it's slow and uninspired.

The world on the other hand, this is where the game shines. The setting is rather unique, the designs are fun, and the dialogues, especially the interactions between main character and his glove, are always fun, sometimes hilarious. Generally, the main character is written ... so uniquely, and the voice actor (in English at least) is perfect for the job.

It's just a shame I do not enjoy ANYTHING else about the game. It clearly took inspiration from some greats like Bioshock, but where that game had an interesting world, a good narrative, a great sense of exploration as well as fun enemy encounters and different "biomes" to go through, Atomic Heart is pretty much the same from start to finish.

The combat encounters are uninspired and character movement is just ... not it. It's ponderous, while making you fight hyper agile enemies. The enemies themselves are almost all bullet sponges, the weapons you get are mostly boring standard fair. This is a hyper stylized future-past with a Soviet touch ... what do you get? A shotgun, an axe, a pistol, a Kalashnikov, a rocket launcher ... etc. And none of them actually do anything remotely different than what you'd expect. The upgrades are boring as hell as well. Your standard "more damage, more accuracy, less recoil" stuff. No alternate firing modes, nothing. It's more like ... Half Life (the first one, mind you), but in bad. But then it has the "plasmids" like in Bioshock ... only they're boring and bad here as well. An electro shock ... is the one you'll use most of the time, because robots + electricity = bad. Then, you can insert capsules into every weapon to do additional elemental damage + effect (fire, electricity and ice), but they're spent pretty quickly with most weapons.
There's also an energy weapon (and a "stronger" one later), but they feel kinda useless against anything but very basic enemies ... I'd rather just craft the ammo, truth be told ...

Stealth is a farce ... so let's not even talk about it. You will not use it. Ever. Do yourself a favor and enable "auto complete quick time events" or whatever it's called.

The worst part about it, though, is the constant rifling through every ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ piece of scenery for vital resources you need to upgrade your character and weapons ... and CRAFT NEW WEAPONS, because in this game you just don't find any weapons, you have to craft them yourself, which takes an inordinate amount of resources ... without you knowing whether the weapon will even be any good or not. The game devolves into "press the ALT key to slowly scan your surroundings for lootable elements" ... constantly. Then "hold the F key to rifle through 100 cabinets in each room, but ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ if the angle is even a bit off!" ... and you do this every few seconds.

The limited inventory is another point of contention ... you can at least gather everything, but if your inventory is full with useless stuff (which you have NO control over out and about in the game world), then you simply cannot pick up more ammo to use RIGHT NOW. Instead it gets sent to your cache. It's rare that you can't pick up ANY ammo, but the difference between 500 Kalash rounds and 100 is quite substantial, obviously. I wish ammo wouldn't clutter your ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ inventory. Or health items. Just have the player pick x weapons to bring with them if you really need to, but overall I'd say, just get rid of the damn inventory altogether!

The level design of the campaign itself is actually rather fine. It's got a decent amount of combat vs. "puzzles", and the story keeps going. Where the game just slows to a crawl is whenever you need to get to the next level via the overworld ... that damn overworld with its constantly respawning enemies where you can, technically, farm enemies forever for resources ... so, you could also just cheat them in if you don't want to waste your time and sanity. The little door puzzles, though, they can ♥♥♥♥ right off. These are various mini games you need to complete in order to unlock a door, sometimes the classic Fallout lockpicking, but there are a few others ... all equally annoying.

The "optional" levels, though ... are so unbelievably boring. They're all puzzle levels involving gravity and spinning plates, and ♥♥♥♥ ... but if you don't do them, you won't really get ANY upgrades for your weapons, which means you're even weaker than you already are. And especially the boss fights are unbearably spongy even WITH max. upgrades ... They're not even hard, they just take like 10 minutes each.

Grabbing and moving items, usually small "balls" is atrocious. The item is always offset to your left, and thanks to the physics you have to awkwardly angle yourself through doors and around corners to actually get it through ... and don't get me started on shooting the damn thing without a cross-hair ... you basically free aim, but not center-mass ... but again very awkwardly offset and angled.

Overall, I feel like I would've enjoyed this more just watching a YT story walkthrough ... which is a shame, because I WAS looking forward to this ... and then I bought all the DLC, because I got it via Humble Choice, and thought "what could possibly go wrong?" ... Well, sometimes I'm an idiot. I'd only get this during a deep deep sale after you've played the demo and have decided you can put up with the boring gameplay and the bad gunplay.

Oh, and there's almost no auto saves, no quick saves, either, you can only really save at save stations ... so be prepared to replay larger sections of a level when you inevitably fall to your death or die for some other reason, because the controls ♥♥♥♥♥♥ you again. Why? What is the reason for not allowing quick save? ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ Half Life did it like 30 years ago! It's fine!

Not sure about the accents, either. This is past WW2 era Soviet Union, yet the locals have ALL sorts of American accents. Generic English, sure, for the sake of the game, but the various accents are putting me off.

As someone that loves Bioshock-esque games, this game is truly right up that alley. It definitely does not have the best dialogue but for a studio that appears to have given this a go on their first try, I'll allow them the quirkiness. It's not blisteringly bad, it's more charming. They know it's not perfect and they're rolling with it, which only adds to the charm. The voice acting isn't bad technically and the characters are certainly unique. I dunno, I like the charm of this game, it's not trying to be better than it is and it knows it isn't perfect and takes full advantage of being as weird as possible at times. So yeah, I'll definitely rate this 7, maybe 8 just for taking a truly unique direction and running with it without looking back. The story only benefits from it.

Mick Gordon also slays with the soundtrack.

Of course compared to other Bioshock-esque games, this ranks maybe around 4th:
Bioshock
Prey
Dishonored
Atomic Heart

And honestly, just making 4th is an achievement in and of itself. I'd recommend this game to people who just want a fun time in a bonkers world, that aren't put off by puzzles. Honestly the game is 35% puzzles.

This game requires the DLCs in order to feel narratively satisfying at all. The final 2 dlc are essential in my opinion. It elevates the game in so many ways that I'd like to raise it's rating to 8 but it would disqualify the qualms I have with the base game. The game definitely should not be an open world, explorative shooter. It needs to be a linear campaign so it can focus more on its very unique and interesting story. Atomic Heart is genuinely one of the best deep dives into transhumanism, Bolshevism, and scientism that I have ever had the enjoyment to experience. You experience the perspective of P-3 as he navigates the Armageddon of a robot social experiment that went awry in order to uncover the mystery of what the Atomic Heart program really is. The combat, environment, and story are all A+ and, while the humor can be off-putting to some, the rest of the game is well worth the investment. I won't spoil anything more, so I recommend the game to anyone who enjoys action games with deeper themes. 8/10, would play again.

[I]Atomic Heart[/I] seemed like vaporware for a long time. This was, with hindsight, unwarranted, but from the trailers the project seemed impossibly good, and with that overly ambitious. When it was clear that the game was actually really real, skepticism continued. This must just be a small scope, the whole game can't be like this, and so on.

[I]Atomic Heart[/I] came, and it kept its style throughout. It's not a particular complex game, this is in many ways a standard shooter with standard weapons - but the draw of it came from its mix of base shooter gameplay with an intriguing world design and brilliant artstyle. The trailers, surprisingly, didn't oversell.

Brightly colored Soviet atompunk, humanoid-but-not-quite robotics, just the general grandiosity - looking at [I]Atomic Heart[/I] is a delight in itself. It's a world that yearns to be explored, much like Rapture did. 

Gameplay itself is punchy and varied. The multitude of enemy designs are backed by uniqueness of engagement. This level of variety is somewhat rare - while you as the player character have a rather typical loadout of weapons enemies do much more than just stand at range and shoot at you. You'll be swarmed, displaced, denied, and chased. [I]Atomic Heart[/I] doesn't let you sit back and play casually, your attention is constantly demanded and the result is a dynamic experience despite you just holding a generic assault rifle.

Writing is mostly acceptable. The narrative is, unfortunately, less interesting than the background lore.

Where issues sometimes arise for others is the characterization of the protagonist. [I]Atomic Heart[/I] had options it could take for its tone - this could be a serious, emotional game where the shiny background serves as a contrast, or it could lean into the color and make the game a wacky fun fest. [I]Atomic Heart[/I] chose the more ambitious endeavor with trying to integrate both.

P3 is very much an '80s American macho man stuck in a futurized Soviet aesthetic. He's campy and fun, but thankfully keeps mum during more serious aspects of the game. The plot is inextricably tied to him and has major consequences for his future, and the resulting juxtaposition is likely what is at the root of the most common criticism of the game.

I disagree that this is an issue. In many ways [I]Atomic Heart[/I] takes its setting and plays it straight - what are the extensions of Soviet aesthete and morality if scarcity was no longer relevant, what impact would this wondrous development of automation hold? It also satirizes these elements - the complete authoritarianism, the devaluation of people, the pure bureaucracy and self-aggrandizement. 

It's a game where we have both enslaved minds and sexual vending machines. Its serious in some ways and camp in others. Have fun with it.

ATOMIC HEART is a (9/10) game; would recommend. 
(I PLAYED ON ONLY LOCAL MALFUNCTION/MEDIUM.) 

Base game: (8/10) The Base game is fun; it has its quirks. It shows a good amount of every mechanic. Testing grounds are a fun Dungeon/side quest mechanic, gunplay is good, none of the guns are unusable, Unlike most games, all guns have a purpose. The upgrade mechanic is simple but still has a fun mechanic to it. The BOSSES are a 50/50 split: half are great fun and somewhat hard, and the other half are boring. The final BOSS fight is great. 

DLC—ANNIHILATION INSTINCT: (6/10) The new guns are good and fun. New enemies had some fun new mechanics. The puzzles are a good bit of fun; the new fast travel mechanic was a good addition. The BOSS fights were hard and just not that fun. 

DLC - TRAPPED IN LIMBO: (5/10) The sliding/surfing parkour is fun, For the first 2 hours, the checkpoint system is not that good. Combat is fun, and the mini-games were a fun side quest. The BOSSES were not fun at all. All the skins were good fun and wacky, but I did not like the MP/pistol skin; it made the iron sights unpleasant to use. 

DLC - ENCHANTMENT UNDER THE SEA: (7/10) First of all, I love the new melee. The new enemies are harder than most other DLC enemies. The puzzles are pretty easy/simple. The final boss fight is not bad but also not great; it's still fun though. 

DLC - BLOOD ON CRYSTAL: (7/10) The final DLC has a majority of the weapons. It brings back most of the hard enemies and adds some fun new ones. The new element system is fun to deal with the new enemies. The FINAL BOSS FIGHT is fun, and if you get caught off guard, you can lose easily.

The "Russian Bioshock" comparisons don't do the game any favors, outside of the gimmick of wielding powers with one hand and a gun in the other, the similarities stop there outside of surface-level observations. Still, it remains a competent game with good ideas, sadly brought down mostly by a poor variety in enemy design and a bad open-world that you'll be better served speeding through to get to the actual main and side missions (which consist mostly of fun puzzles and a couple of arenas). Visually the game can look amazing in some setpieces and scenes but sadly most of your gameplay will be spent inside sterile corridors, the "dungeon" after the intro is a perfect example of this, it's a terrible first impression of the game.
The combat can sometimes be fun, but in most cases you'll end up being jerked around and spammed with attacks that are barely reactable even when spamming dash. Powers have synergies, in theory, but you won't have the time to do the setup and it's way more effective spamming AoE like mass telekinesis for big damage. Bosses are a lot better in that regard but are quite scarce. Also note the last one in Enchantment Under the Sea is pretty hard (took me 3-4 tries).

Writing is mostly light-hearted so you will be disappointed if you look for something 100% serious. The "face" of the game, The Twins, barely appear in the first game but they're more present in the last 2 DLCs for reasons I won't spoil. Devs also obviously noticed lots of people liked their design so they started insisting on it more.

The story DLCs (bar Lost in Limbo which is an arcade oriented DLC you can skip unless you like cs_surf) are a lot better in everything regarding the points I cited above. The open-world is gone, there's new enemies, better level design (to navigate and visually). It's still not perfect, but if you take the edition with all DLCs it's a solid 6.5 or 7/10 game, considering it's pretty cheap on sale regularly I recommend it.
There's a lot of room for improvement so Atomic Heart 2 or The Cube could end up being great if they fix every underwhelming aspect of this game.

[h1] Atomic Heart - The Good, The Bad, The Miss Opportunity[/h1]

Back when Atomic Heart showcasing its demo in 2019, i’m actually hyped that we will have another Bioshock-esque game with different tone and premise. Then 2023 happened, Atomic Heart launched and I still remembered it got mixed reviews even to this day. When I finally bought and spent 43 hours to complete the campaign and all 4 DLCs, I could say i have mixed feeling about this game. It ain’t the worst and not the greatest but hear me out.

==============================================

[h2]Gameplay – They didn’t learn what makes Bioshock fun[/h2]

I could say Atomic Heart main game is kinda Far Cry and Bioshock hybrid, which combine Far Cry open world and Bioshock like power ups by the Glove mechanics. The open world is full of surveillance camera which really painful to play in early hour of the game, and there is no way to disable it which is very annoying. I know in near end game camera is not an issue anymore but in the open world section is really tedious to play with limited arsenal. 
The Glove mechanic is interesting and the skill tree rewards exploration and combat, but the power variety feels lacking, especially without a fire ability in the base game. Limiting the glove to only two active powers also feels unnecessary. A power wheel system like Bioshock would’ve been much more convenient and could’ve fixed the confusing cartridge mechanic and excessive item pickups. 
Still, I enjoyed the platforming as a change of pace, and despite the small weapon selection, every gun feels useful.

Gameplay 7.8/10

==============================================

[h2]Story – Atomic Heart is What is Wrong with Modern Game[/h2]

I think I will agree with people who said Atomic Heart’s story is not really that good, i mean narratively. The game has a strong story premise but suffered with really bad bad screen writing. Every dialog felt like written by middle schooler, and everyone is shouting whenever they talk, oh and every character in this game is really comical. Nechaev as protagonist is really unlikeable, a thick-headed soldier which really barely change or learn anything throughout the game. I think whenever he delivers a line is really weird and out of place like anyone else in the game. 
The writing department really need to learn how to make a mature game with a mature story, and not Marvel Cinematic Universe type of dialog which shouting their line sequentially. Making these character too comical not only by visual but by their line, also make us, players didn’t care about what happen to this character.
The turn point event like *spoiler Nikolai sacrifice felt flat, because throughout the game his screentime he just too timid and nervous about everything even he is a genius engineer, like c’mon can we have a good scientist not so quirky or character that too timid just because he is a genius engineer? It makes me sick. It makes me sick! How to fix that? If you want to kill a character by a sacrifice, make sure he gets enough screentime and having good conversation with the protagonist or player so their death not so fell flat. What do you think I felt after beating this game. I FELT NOTHING, YOU LOSE, GOOD DAY SIR. I couldn’t feel either happy or sad because how flat and comical all the character is, I couldn’t goon to any character because how they left bitter taste after the story ended, and IMO the only good character in this game is NORA. 
The pacing is also rough. Vavilov Complex is way too long for an intro section; it took me 6 hours before the game finally felt like it opened up properly.
Story: 4/10
==============================================
[h2]Graphics - All Budget to Build the Atmosphere[/h2]
First of all, who the hell design Filatrova in this game, she felt out of the place in 1950s Soviet. Second, when you know you couldn’t afford making a realistic body model and texture, so play with a good art direction to cover your limitation. I can say the atmosphere in the game is good but all human character is lacking especially all running character in the game they felt cheap, and IDK why Nastya feel like from a porn game. Also this game lacking enviromental interaction.
But the human characters look inconsistent and sometimes cheap. Some designs feel completely out of place, and the animations can be awkward. Instead of chasing realism, I think the game would benefit more from a stronger stylized art direction.
Still, the environmental artists carried this game hard.
Graphic: 8/10
==============================================
[h2]Music – It’s ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ Slaps[/h2]
Just search the OST on YouTube. Enough said.
Music is: 10/10

==============================================

[h2]Performance – No UE5 ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥, but Still has UE Feel[/h2]

The game actually runs quite well on my PC. On my RTX 2060S, i could reach to 110 FPS on High-Medium setting in 1440p and dips to 50-ish when there’s ton of object flying around in combat. I honor the devs to not switch to UE5 after a long development cycle in UE 4, but if you familiar with UE games especially UE 4 generation, you might be could tell Atomic Heart is an Unreal Game by its lighting preset that gives too much gamma clarity. The minimum spec really spot on how optimize this game and I find it funny as ex GTX 960 user myself, based on my experience, that graphic card really could handle any UE4 games with ease. Anyway Credit to the devs for sticking with UE4 instead of forcing a UE5 transition mid-development.

Performance wise 8/10

==============================================
[h2]DLC – They Almost Fix the Combat Issue of the Main Game[/h2]

I could tell they really listen to the critics about the too much pick ups especially gun cartridges, by swapping them to glove mechanics. But like i mentioned before why they still keep two slots only with those additional mechanics. Also, why they didn’t make the hookshot as separate mechanic that integrated to the glove rather than making it use a slot to the glove mechanics. I love the DLC keeping the game linear but in some sections there’s too much closed space and corridors making it barely any exploration at all. And yeah, the second DLC Suck, then I’ll give them score like this.
DLC#1 – Annihilation Instinct: 5/10
DLC#2 – Trapped in Limbo: 3/10
DLC#3 – Enchantment Under The Sea: 8/10
DLC#4 – Blood on Crystal: 6.5/10

I was initially apprehensive about Atomic Heart because of some of the criticisms I heard. When I tried the demo and then ultimately bought the Gold Edition on sale after the release of its last DLC, I found that I loved the game. It surprised me in many ways and made me much more excited for Atomic Heart 2. 

Positives:
- Art Direction: It's phenomenal and unique with a lot of personality that works together cohesively to craft a believable and interesting world. 
- Music: I love when devs put a lot of effort into a game's soundtrack, and Mundfish definitely did here. There are many memorable tracks that I actually enjoy listening to outside the game. 
- Story: I found the story very interesting and decently paced. You absolutely have to have the DLCs to get the entirety of the story, but the journey was entertaining and enjoyable. 
- Characters: This one might be more up to personal taste. One of the aspects that initially made me apprehensive was the criticism surrounding P-3's personality. In the very beginning of the game, I was worried because he can definitely come off as sort of campy and dismissive. However, as the game went on, I actually found myself really liking P-3 and the other characters that are introduced throughout the story. All of their voice acting is great, and to me, Atomic Heart was at its best when P-3 was talking to/interacting with the characters. Much of the journey can feel lonely, so when you finally do get instances (though limited) where you can interact with other characters, it was nice. I genuinely hope P-3 and the other characters will play major, if not main, roles in the sequel. 
- Puzzles: I personally enjoyed the puzzles. The base game has a solid number of puzzle dungeons that do require some trial and error and spatial awareness, but none were so difficult that you couldn't figure them out after a few minutes. 

Mixed: 
- Combat. Overall, I enjoyed combat, but there were some things that frustrated me. The game puts you in various scenarios where you're facing a very mobile enemy or group of enemies. To succeed in the encounter, you have to be just as mobile, yet your own movement at times could feel sort of slow/cumbersome. Also, every enemy type has an attack that can knock you down. When you get swarmed, you can get knocked down repeatedly and it can be a bit aggravating. 
- Presentation: So the actual cutscenes and camera direction is great, but there were numerous instances where the lip sync was off or the HUD would display during cutscenes. Nothing so criminal as to ruin the game, but just enough to challenge scene immersion. 
- Music timing: Again, the music itself is fantastic. There are just instances where it will start and stop depending on the combat circumstances.
- Dialogue timing: There were instances where a character would start speaking right when a frenetic, loud combat sequence starts. Consequently, you can't really absorb what they're saying. 
- Performance: Most of the time, the game ran great at max settings. However, there were instances where I'd experience a frame drop or freeze. I had 1 game crash throughout my 50 or so hours. 

Negatives:
- The open world: in the base game after a certain point you're put into an open world that has numerous other dungeons placed throughout. These dungeons are important if you want to get specific upgrades/blueprints for your weapons. Yet it's everything in between the dungeons and story points that makes it oppressive. 
       - You cannot permanently clear enemies. I get the narrative and thematic reasoning behind this, but for gameplay, it makes travel in the open world very aggravating at times and combat ultimately a waste of resources. The game tries to encourage stealth, however, triggering a detection is way too easy. And when you try to just run through, you will get absolutely swarmed by enemies to the point it will drive you crazy sometimes. There are things you can do to mitigate enemy spawns but they're ultimately still temporary. It would have added a lot to the sense of progression if players could feel like they could clear an area, or at the very least significantly reduce the enemy population. 
      - Aside from the optional dungeons for blueprints, there really isn't much reason to explore the open world aside from looking for resources. I wish there were interesting points of interests or interactions. Maybe something like saving NPCs or more nuanced environmental storytelling to make this structure stand out from that structure. 
- Trapped in Limbo DLC: I tried to push myself through it but gave up. I liked that a certain character got much more dialogue in this, but the DLC adds this platforming and rail-riding gameplay that's just frustrating. Perhaps if the level designs were better, that gameplay wouldn't have been bad, but the level design genuinely feels like its there to annoy you and waste your time rather than challenge you. I liked the idea behind the DLC, but its overall design and gameplay truly undermine it. 

Addendum on the DLCs:
For the full story, you will want to pick them up. If the Gold Edition is on sale, absolutely buy it. It's a steal.
- Just note that for Annihilation Instinct, while an enjoyable experience, it's based on a certain ending and the introduction of the DLC really leaves you narratively confused because it doesn't outright explain it. Also, it has a bug with an objective marker. You have to collect a certain number of an item and the objective marker just doesn't update after a certain point. To be as non-spoiler as possible: you have to find an elevator that goes outside. 
- As noted above, Trapped In Limbo is rough. Try it and get your own opinion of it, but know what to expect. 
- Enchantment Under the Sea and Blood on Crystal continue the actual story of the base game and build upon what makes the game great. You meet several great characters and get a conclusion that also sets up Mundfish's upcoming games.

Overall: I loved Atomic Heart and would strongly recommend it. If you pick it up on sale, you're getting a steal. It's a great first entry for a studio that solidly sets up a game world they obviously want to build upon.

This is one of the most schizophrenic games I've ever played.

On the one hand, the gameplay is broadly enjoyable, but on the other, almost every interesting idea was unceremoniously lifted from a better game. 

On the one hand, the story is fairly interesting, and on the other it's clumsy, confused, and can't work out whether it wants to pipe the Soviet Union off or condemn its values, not to mention the translation is excruciating at times. 

On the one hand it runs whisper-quiet, considering the graphical fidelity, but on the other hand it's a janky, broken mess with multiple soft locks still hanging around three years later. 

On the one hand, the DLCs are kind of interesting, but on the other, they're massively copy-paste in places, and exhibit painfully bad game design.

I got it on sale, and while there was quantity, the quality was massively uneven - so play it safe if you're thinking of buying it, and wait until it's on sale.

A really good game that changes identity about 3-5 times throughout the playthrough. You can tell the devs had to change directions a few times and were never really afforded the opportunity to really nail down what they were originally going for. 
This is not really a Bioshock-like, nor is it much of anything it tries to become, but in that repeated process, it does, eventually, become its own unique thing- especially in the dlcs
I would have LOVED to see Atomic Heart really nail it greater ambitions. It fumbles mechanically, and the voice actors, at least in english, seem to often be given poor direction making a few intended jokes or endearing lines fall flat on delivery only to make sense on reflection.
Go into this knowing you're in for several moments of genuinely good plot and character writing, and several jarring moments that ruin immersion- but if you can lean in and embrace the -game- its pretty fun.

Played through the game and DLC on the hardest difficulty, the DLC is a continuation of the story but it isn't connected to the main game in a single save file more like stand alone episodes.

The main game is similar to Bioshock except you can only have two active abilities equipped at once and you can only use one at a time but you can re-spec at any point so it's not too bad. The weapons are pretty mediocre some are better than others with a higher DPS, and you can see that in the DLC where those weapons don't appear. The game is a Pseudo open world but it's more like an "over world" that connects the levels and the open world itself is very sparse with little to do or see, all the loot is just crafting materials to upgrade weapons so nothing feels like a unique discovery. There's a car that you can use to get around but you will never need to go back to other parts of the over world because the entire game is very linear and if you're playing on easier difficulties it's very short as well. 

The DLC is even more linear and strips down mechanics and weapons although I would say that it's to the games benefit since the base game had a lot of chaff and instead focuses on the games strong points such as the story and puzzles.

I really like The graphics even though they have a very UE5 aesthetic if you know what I mean. The music was really cool, it makes generic encounters more hype than they actually are and I might but the OST, lots of Russian metal.

Overall the game feels disjointed and poorly planned, the main game ends on a cliff hanger continued by the DLC each of which brings new mechanics and weapons while getting rid of the old ones, felt like it was all over the place gameplay wise but the story was really nice and the characters, while initially annoying, really grew on me. I really look forward to what the developers make next.

Also this game has really bad DRM, had my internet go down for a second and wouldn't let me play, get rid of that in the sequel.
This is a very fun and enjoyable game. I recommend anyone picking it up  and giving it a go. I look forward to future projects from Mundfish.
